summary:
Giant cell tumors can contain multiple cell types including osteoclastic and mononucleated elements; they often remain localized but occasionally lead to pulmonary metastases despite their generally benign nature. Therefore, \(\boxed{\text{C}}\) is likely not accurate since these tumors typically involve more than just giant cells alone during growth phases. Agent1 concludes by identifying \( \boxed{\text{C}} \) as the exception among provided options based on reviewed facts concerning the histological makeup and behavior patterns characteristic of this type of lesion. 

The summary has been condensed into two clear sentences focusing only on critical aspects related directly back to evaluating each choice against known medical literature specifics pertinent here—cellular components involved versus likelihoods associated with distant spread or local persistence characteristics typical within such neoplasms classified broadly under 'giant-cell' nomenclature used clinically today.
